articles.title: History of Argentine Tango
articles.excerpt: Argentine tango emerged in late 19th-century Buenos Aires and Montevideo through immigrants and workers, later gaining European popularity in the 1970s.
articles.author: Sonja

content.introduction: At the end of the 19th century, the dances and music of black slaves, seasonal workers, and European immigrants merged with those of gauchos in the Río de la Plata region.

sections.title: Early Development (1890-1900)
sections.text: Men experimented with steps like canyengue when women were scarce, accompanied by minimal instrumentation—typically guitar, violin, or flute.

sections.title: Argentine Boom (1930-1950)
sections.text: The "época de oro" (golden era) saw widespread adoption. Radio broadcasting significantly amplified tango's cultural dominance throughout Argentina.

sections.title: European Adoption
sections.text: A sanitized "ballroom tango" initially gained European acceptance, though the authentic Argentine style didn't resurface there until the 1970s.

sections.title: Modern Revival
sections.text: Contemporary tango culture features dance schools, milongas, festivals, marathons, and "tango tourism" across Europe, with Buenos Aires maestros performing internationally.

content.conclusion: UNESCO inscribed Argentine Tango on the Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2009, recognizing its cultural significance.

articles.title: The different styles of Argentine tango
articles.excerpt: Explores various Argentine tango styles including Canyengue, Milonguero, and Tango de Salon with details on poses and music.
articles.author: Sonja

content.introduction: Argentine tango thus refers to a broad spectrum of dance styles. Yet most dancers resist categorical labels, preferring to describe their approach as the tango of their neighbourhood or city.

sections.title: Canyengue (1920s-1930s)
sections.text: Short, staccato steps with close embrace and bent knees. Music in 2/4 rhythmic tempo. Notable orchestras: Francisco Canaro, Roberto Firpo.

sections.title: Tango Orillero
sections.text: Upright posture with playful, expressive movements. Revived by Rodolfo Cieri circa 1990. Associated with working-class neighborhoods.

sections.title: Tango de Salon
sections.text: Elegant, social style for ballroom settings with closed embrace (abrazo cerrado). Notable composers: Carlos Di Sarli, Miguel Calo, Osvaldo Pugliese.

sections.title: Tango Milonguero
sections.text: Downtown Buenos Aires style (also called Estilo del Centro). Developed due to crowded dance floors requiring compact movements. Music preference: rhythmic pieces by Juan D'Arienzo, Tanturi, Biagi. Popularized globally by Pedro "Tete" Rusconi.

sections.title: Tango Villa Urquiza
sections.text: 1940s-1950s Buenos Aires neighborhood style. Features upright posture with subtle V-position, independent hip rotation. Often called "salon-style tango" internationally.

sections.title: Tango Nuevo
sections.text: Modern, experimental with open embrace. Features volcadas and creative figures. Founders: Gustavo Naveira, Mariano "Chicho" Frumboli. Other pioneers: Norberto "El Pulpo" Esbrés, Fabián Salas, Sebastian Arce, Mariana Montes.

sections.title: Tango Fantasia
sections.text: Developed in the 1950s and popularized by Juan Carlos Copes. Fully choreographed stage performance style that incorporates ballet elements and requires 9 cm heels.

sections.title: Tango Escenario
sections.text: Designed specifically for performances and stage shows.

content.conclusion: Christine Denniston's research from The Meaning of Tango reveals that although their dance styles varied widely, Golden Age dancers all simply called what they danced 'Tango de Salon.'

articles.title: Argentine tango & ballroom tango, similarities and differences
articles.excerpt: Comparative analysis of Argentine and ballroom tango traditions, highlighting distinctions within each dancing approach.
articles.author: Sven

content.introduction: The piece traces tango's emergence in late 19th-century Buenos Aires and Montevideo, where the dance and music of black slaves, seasonal workers and immigrants blended with gaucho traditions.

sections.title: Argentine Tango Characteristics
sections.text: Described as "a dance that moves"—one remaining unfinished and continuously evolving. Features closed embrace (abrazo cerrado) or open embrace (abrazo abierto). The distinguishing feature is improvisation: Argentine tango represents "a non-verbal dialogue between leader and follower," contrasting sharply with choreographed dances.

sections.title: Six Distinctive Styles
sections.text: Canyengue, Orillero, Milonguero, Tango Salon, Tango Fantasia, and Tango Nuevo each reflect different historical periods and evolutionary stages.

sections.title: Ballroom Tango Characteristics
sections.text: Derived from Argentine tango but standardized as a polished, European-influenced competition dance. Maintains strict tempo of 31-33 bars per minute. Features open frame with space between partners and slightly bent knees. Ballroom splits into "American style" and "International Style."

sections.title: Key Distinctions
sections.text: Argentine tango emphasizes improvisation, intimate connection, and musical interpretation freedom, whereas ballroom tango prioritizes standardized technique, competitive structure, and stylized execution.

content.conclusion: While both styles share ancestral connections, they diverge fundamentally in their approach to music, connection, and performance.

articles.title: The difference between tango, milonga & vals
articles.excerpt: Overview of three distinct musical styles within Argentine tango practice, each possessing unique characteristics.
articles.author: Sven

content.introduction: Argentine tango includes three distinct dance variants: tango, vals, and milonga. Despite expectations, dancers use the same combinations across all three styles. The distinction lies in which movements suit each musical style.

sections.title: Tango
sections.text: Functions as a walking dance emphasizing controlled weight transfer and dramatic pauses. The rhythm allows dancers to perform extended movements, creating an emotional quality through deliberate stops and restarts.

sections.title: Vals
sections.text: Features a continuous 1-2-3 rhythm that eliminates pauses typical of tango, resulting in circular movements and a cheerful character. The rhythmic consistency requires shorter, more dynamic pushes compared to tango.

sections.title: Milonga
sections.text: Characterized by its distinctive rhythm and rapid tempo, making it "one of the most difficult dances to master." With minimal pauses and quick changes, dancers make smaller steps with distributed weight between both legs.

content.conclusion: Mastery requires listening to music while practicing physically, developing technique through repeated experimentation across all three dance forms.

articles.title: 5 tips for the best tango shoes
articles.excerpt: Good tango shoes improve dance technique, reduce injuries and increase the fun in Argentine Tango.
articles.author: Sonja

content.introduction: The article explains why dedicated tango shoes matter compared to regular sneakers, emphasizing the improved feeling and performance when wearing proper dance footwear.

sections.title: 1. Light Construction & Support
sections.text: Quality dance shoes are lighter than city shoes and provide superior ankle/knee support. They feature chamois leather soles that grip without sticking, reducing injury risk.

sections.title: 2. Balance & Heel Height
sections.text: Proper weight distribution improves stability. Recommended minimums: 5cm heels for women, 1.5-2cm for men. Heel height enables proper ball-of-foot dancing technique essential for Argentine tango's characteristic movements and pivots.

sections.title: 3. Women's vs. Men's Shoes
sections.text: Both require flexible forefeet and secure heel enclosure. Key difference: men's shoes have rounded (vs. pointed) toes to prevent injuring partners.

sections.title: 4. Sole Materials
sections.text: Options include chamois leather, suede, rubber, or combinations depending on dance floor surfaces (wood, marble, tile). Suede soles can be brushed clean after use.

sections.title: 5. Fit Considerations
sections.text: Shoes must enclose feet securely without sliding. Try multiple pairs across different stores before purchasing. Quality pairs typically cost €120–€180.

content.conclusion: Good tango shoes improve dance technique, reduce injuries and increase the fun in Argentine Tango.

articles.title: Why You Should Learn Argentine Tango
articles.excerpt: Argentine tango represents one of the most sophisticated couple dances you can learn. Discover why tango is perfect for all ages and backgrounds.
articles.author: Sonja & Sven
articles.imageAlt: Students having fun learning Argentine tango at BE-TANGO

content.introduction: Argentine tango represents one of the most sophisticated couple dances you can learn. It welcomes participants of all ages and backgrounds, regardless of prior dance experience.

sections.title: A Social Dance for Everyone
sections.text: Tango functions as a social dance with regular gatherings called milongas where practitioners practice dancing, talk, and share drinks in welcoming environments. Brussels offers nightly opportunities for social tango dancing.

sections.title: Improvisation: The Heart of Tango
sections.text: As a folkloric dance form, Argentine tango allows leaders to interpret the music and improvise the dance steps. Followers embellish movements with techniques like boleos and ganchos, enabling dancers to be creative and invent their own steps.

sections.title: Beyond Tango: Milonga & Vals
sections.text: Learn three dances: Tango for emotional expression, Milonga for fast rhythmical dancing, and Vals for circular flowing movements to 1-2-3 rhythm.

sections.title: An International Dance Community
sections.text: Tango functions as an international dance enabling connections with people from everywhere and of all ages. Major cities worldwide host tango parties and festivals.

content.conclusion: Whether you're looking to improve your coordination, meet new people, discover a creative outlet, or simply try something new and exciting, Argentine tango has something special to offer.

articles.title: Tango Events in Brussels & Around
articles.excerpt: Discover the best tango events, milongas, and festivals in Brussels and Belgium. From La Milonguita to Brussels Tango Festival.
articles.author: Sonja & Sven
articles.imageAlt: Tango dancing and classes in Brussels

content.introduction: Belgium's vibrant tango scene offers dancers the opportunity to go out dancing every night of the week in most major cities.

sections.title: Featured Milongas
sections.text: La Milonguita (Rue Montoyer), Tango Bar (Rue de Dublin), El Dorado (Grimbergen), El Regalo (Sint-Gillis), Pianofabriek, and Maison du Peuple offer regular dancing opportunities.

sections.title: Brussels Tango Festival
sections.text: With over 15 years of tradition, this international event utilizes prestigious venues including Concert Noble and the Grand Place, featuring workshops with world-renowned teachers.

sections.title: Antwerp Tango Festival
sections.text: Celebrating more than 10 years, hosted at Zaal Athena, attracting dancers from throughout Belgium and neighboring countries.

sections.title: Tango Marathons
sections.text: La Cita de los Amigos (winter at Brigittines, summer in Spa) and Palace Cita (historic cinema venue) offer intensive weekend dancing experiences.

content.conclusion: The welcoming community, excellent venues, and frequent events make Belgium a wonderful place to pursue your passion for Argentine tango.

articles.title: International Tango Events & Milongas Abroad
articles.excerpt: Where can you travel for tango? Discover the best international tango festivals and marathons across Europe and beyond.
articles.author: Sven

content.introduction: Major cities across Europe and the world host tango festivals, milongas, and marathons, enabling dancers to combine a city trip with their passion for tango.

sections.title: Top European Festivals
sections.text: Belgrade Tango Encuentro (cozy atmosphere), Ljubljana Tango Festival (renowned maestros), Mediterranean Summer Tango (Poreč, Croatia), Tango Amadeus (Vienna), and Tango Alchemie (Prague) offer unique experiences.

sections.title: Must-Visit Marathons
sections.text: Prague Tango Marathon (February), Copenhagen Tango Marathon, Palace Cita Brussels, La Tosca (Tuscany), Pensalobien (Milan), and La Cita de los Amigos offer intensive dancing weekends.

sections.title: Planning Your Tango Travel
sections.text: Consider city attractions, event size, music style, dancing level requirements, and timing when choosing international tango events.

sections.title: Resources
sections.text: Tangopolix.com provides comprehensive festival information while TangoMarathons.com lists complete marathon calendars with registration details.

content.conclusion: The international tango community welcomes dancers worldwide. Each city and event has its unique character, offering endless opportunities for discovery and connection through dance.
